data.title: GOP blocks war power limits again — where are the checks and balances
data.description:
For the fourth time since the conflict with Iran began, Senate Republicans blocked an effort to limit President Trump’s war powers — essentially choosing, again, not to force a clearer line between executive authority and congressional oversight.   

And here’s the thing about war powers debates in Washington. They often sound procedural, but what’s actually at stake is very real: who decides when America goes to war, how long it lasts, and how much it costs.  

On paper, this is about a resolution. In reality, it’s about whether Congress is willing to assert its constitutional role at all. Do something, anything to be sure the president doesn’t continue to make decisions about conflict without engaging them.  

We’re now weeks into a war with no clearly defined endgame. Oil prices have surged, global shipping routes are under pressure, and the Pentagon is reportedly preparing a $200 billion funding request. That’s not a small, abstract number. That’s taxpayer money tied to a war that lawmakers themselves admit they don’t fully understand.  

Even inside the Republican Party, there are signs of discomfort.  

Sen. Lisa Murkowski put it plainly after hearing from constituents back home. She said, “The answer on most of this is, I don’t know… I want to know some of the answers to the questions Alaskans are asking me. I think we need to have open hearings.”  

That’s not a partisan talking point. That’s a sitting Republican senator acknowledging a basic gap: the people funding the war and the lawmakers representing them don’t have clear answers.  

Other Republicans have echoed similar concerns, calling for more briefings, more transparency, and a clearer strategy. Some have even admitted they haven’t been fully briefed since early in the conflict.   

The problem is, that concern is not translating into action.  

When it comes time to vote, the majority of Republicans are still siding with the president, effectively allowing the conflict to continue without new constraints. That tells you something important about today’s political environment: even when there are doubts, party alignment is still outweighing institutional responsibility.  

And Democrats, for their part, are trying to force the issue, arguing that this is exactly what the War Powers Act was designed for — to prevent a president from unilaterally dragging the country into a prolonged conflict.  

So where does that leave us?  

In a place where the war continues, the costs are rising, and the oversight is, well, optional.  

This isn’t just about Trump or Iran. It’s about precedent. Because every time Congress chooses not to assert its authority, it becomes easier for the next president — Republican or Democrat — to bypass it, too.  

When will lawmakers act on their concerns? Isn’t that the whole point of having checks and balances?  

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Watch live: RFK Jr testifies before House Appropriations panel on HHS budget
data.description:
Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. will testify before the House Appropriations Committee on Thursday afternoon on the White House’s fiscal 2027 budget request and the Trump administration’s health policy.

President Trump requested additional funding for the administration’s “Make America Healthy Again” agenda and proposed “eliminating bloated, woke, and inefficient programs that do not advance MAHA goals.”

Kennedy appeared before the House Ways and Means Committee earlier in the day, where he faced criticism over HHS’s revival of a debunked theory linking autism and childhood vaccines, among other controversies.

The hearing is scheduled to begin at 2 p.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: 'Really bad': Ground vehicle crosses in front of American Airlines plane at Charlotte airport, pilot says
data.description:
CHARLOTTE (WJZY) – A collision was avoided at the Charlotte Douglas International Airport after a vehicle pulled in front of a taxiing plane, according to the airport and American Airlines.

Officials say the CLT Airport ground vehicle reportedly entered the path of an American Airlines aircraft near the gate on Wednesday morning. There was no collision between the two vehicles.

Audio from the cockpit of the American Airlines plane obtained by outlets including ABC News and Simple Flying indicates that the pilot was forced to “slam on our brakes” to avoid hitting the vehicle.

“They just went right in front of us and we nearly hit them,” a pilot can he heard saying.

“Someone’s gotta be notified right away. That was really bad.”

Footage of the incident later shared to Nexstar’s WJZY of the incident appears to show two vehicles — both trucks — driving directly into the path of the plane, which stops. The trucks appear to keep driving.

The FAA, in a statement shared with Nexstar, said the incident occurred in an area of the airport not overseen by air traffic control. The agency is investigating.

American Airlines has since praised its crew for its handling of the incident.

“We’re proud of the professionalism of our crew and the action they took when a ground vehicle not affiliated with American Airlines entered the aircraft’s path on a taxiway,” American Airlines said in a statement.

CLT Airport, too, said it would be looking into the incident.

“Safety and security are top priorities at CLT, and this incident is being investigated in accordance with safety standards,” the airport wrote.

Wednesday’s incident occurred less than a month after two Air Canada pilots were killed when their aircraft collided with a fire truck that was crossing the tarmac at LaGuardia Airport in New York. In early April, the pilot of a Frontier Airways jet also claimed that he needed to hit the brakes after two trucks crossed in front of the plane while taxiing.

“It was real close. The closest I have ever seen,” he said in audio posted by ATC.com, the Associated Press reported at the time.

data.title: US Census Bureau releases list of 1,000 most common first names in America
data.description:
AUSTIN (KXAN) — Chances are there’s someone named Michael in your life. It’s the most common first name in the country, according to data just released by the U.S. Census Bureau. One out of every 100 people in the U.S. is named Michael.

The agency released the list of 1,000 most common first names, based on data collected during the 2020 Census.

A total of 3,476,721 Michaels were recorded across the country. John was the only other name to appear more than 3 million times. James, David and Robert round out the top five.

Mary is the most common female name on the list — but seventh overall.

Most names that appear on the list heavily favor one gender — 99.8% of Michaels are male and 99.8% of Marys are female, for example. But many names are more gender neutral.

Harley was the most gender neutral name on the list. The name ranks as the 980th most common in the country, with 43,968 Harleys in total. About 50.8% of those are female, while 49.2% are male.

Emerson, Quinn and Kerry were the next most gender neutral names on the list.

Most common last names

The Census Bureau also released a list of the top 15 most common last names. Smith remains the most common, ahead of Johnson, Williams, Brown and Jones.

Those five names have consistently been the top five over the past four censuses — and even as far back as the 1790 Census, the first in U.S. history.

In more recent years, Hispanic last names have been rising in the ranks. Garcia is currently the sixth-most common last name in the country, while Rodriguez and Martinez also appear in the top 10.

Asian names are also becoming more common. Zhang was listed as a last name about 122,000 times in 2020, a 74% increase over the 2010 Census, making it the “fastest-growing last name” in the country. Liu and Wang round out the top three fastest-growing last names.

data.title: Trump support drops among Catholics after Pope Leo remarks, poll shows
data.description:
(NewsNation) — President Trump’s comments about Pope Leo aren’t sitting well with Catholic voters, a new poll shows.

The poll, conducted late last month, found the president’s approval rating among Catholic voters has dropped to 48%, with 52% expressing disapproval.

Trump’s attacks on Leo began after the pope amplified criticism of war and asserted that God doesn’t bless those who drop bombs. Leo also called Trump’s threat to annihilate Iranian civilization “truly unacceptable.” The president also accused the Chicago-born pontiff of being weak on crime and a captive to the left.

Beyond his spat with the pope, Trump has recently drawn the ire of his supporters for posting, then taking down, an artificial intelligence-generated, Christ-like image of himself.

The poll’s data also indicated just 40% of Catholic respondents approve of Trump’s handling of the conflict, while 60% disapprove.

“What emerges from this convergence of data and events is not simply a decline in approval ratings, but a more profound recalibration,” said Zenit.org’s Jorge Enrique Mujica.

“For many Catholic voters, the question is no longer confined to partisan preference. It touches on the coherence between faith and political judgment, particularly in matters of war, peace, and the moral limits of power.”

data.title: Bannon advises Hegseth to 'tone down' bible talk: 'It steps on what's important'
data.description:
Former White House strategist Steve Bannon advised Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth on Thursday to tone down religious references in briefings on the conflict with Iran, arguing they distract from the operational details outlined by military leaders.

“It was a briefing of precision,” Bannon said on his show “War Room,” lauding Gen. Dan Caine, the chair of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, and Adm. Brad Cooper, the commander of U.S. Central Command, for doing a “magnificent job” explaining the purpose and mechanics of the U.S. blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.  

“My observation or recommendation: I don’t think I would start that with some references to the New Testament,” he added.

Hegseth has used much of his routine Pentagon briefings on the war to complain about the media’s coverage, lashing out at the press corps again on Thursday.  

“As I just can’t help but notice the endless stream of garbage, the relentlessly negative coverage you cannot resist peddling, despite the historic and important success of this effort and the success of our troops,” Hegseth said. “Sometimes it’s hard to figure out what side some of you are actually on.”

He compared some reporters to the Pharisees, a Jewish group frequently portrayed in the Bible as opponents of Jesus.  

“The legacy Trump-hating press, your politically motivated animus for President Trump nearly completely blinds you from the brilliance of our American warriors,” Hegseth said.

The secretary’s religious invocations, including a call for American families to pray for U.S. service members on “bended knee … in the name of Jesus Christ,” have drawn concern from some veterans and experts about possibly causing a divide within the military.

A group of 30 Democrats asked the Defense Department’s inspector general in early March to open an investigation into whether Hegseth’s “extreme religious rhetoric has metastasized into segments of the military chain of command in ways that contravene constitutional protections, departmental rules and standards, or professional military norms.”

Bannon offered his “two cents” on the issue on Thursday, suggesting briefings should be reserved for discussions on the military’s capabilities and strategic goals.

“That’s all the mainstream media is covering right now is Pete’s opening remark about the Gospel according to Mark and the reference to the Pharisees, which is, I think, great. It’s just when are you going to do it? I would not do it because it, it steps on what’s important,” he said. “We ought to tone that down and focus, when we got the Pentagon, let’s have a military briefing.”

data.title: HHS ends $11M contract with Catholic Charities to care for migrant children
data.description:
A Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) sub-agency has decided not to renew an $11 million contract with Catholic Charities, a move that will affect efforts to care for migrant children.

The Office of Refugee Resettlement (ORR) will no longer fund the Catholic Charities shelter run by the Archdiocese of Miami after several years of continuous federal support for the entity. 

The shelter provides housing and care to migrant children who are currently in the country without a parent or guardian. 

“ORR is closing and consolidating unused facilities as the Trump administration continues efforts to stop illegal entry and the smuggling and trafficking of unaccompanied alien children,” HHS press secretary Emily G. Hillard said in a statement to The Hill. 

News of the drawback comes amid President Trump’s tensions with the Catholic Church and repeated swipes at Pope Leo XIV for condemning the Iran war.

The religious non-profit operates the equivalent of a federally funded foster care system with a current daily population of 1,900 unaccompanied minors according to an HHS official.

Comparatively, it housed 22,000 under the Biden administration.

“The U.S. government has abruptly decided to end more than 60 years of relationship with Catholic Charities in the Archdiocese of Miami,” Archbishop Thomas Wenkski wrote in a statement for the Miami Herald editorial board.

“The Archdiocese of Miami’s services for unaccompanied minors have been recognized for their excellence and have served as a model for other agencies throughout the country,” he added.

The final decision of non-renewal was made on Feb. 16, per the HHS official.

The local Catholic Charities organization was contacted in March by the federal government about the cancellation, according to The Miami Herald.

“Our track record in serving this vulnerable population is unmatched. Yet, the Archdiocese of Miami’s Catholic Charities’ services for unaccompanied minors has been stripped of funding and will be forced to shut down within three months,” Wenski said in his statement to the Herald. 

On Thursday, White House senior deputy press secretary Kush Desai denied that the local Catholic Charities organization was recently informed of the cancelled funding. But he said that two Catholic groups were told last year of the Trump administration’s decision not to renew contracts.

“Fake News,” Desai wrote in a post on X, referring to the Miami Herald’s reporting. 

“Last year, 49 charities with federal grants to care for unaccompanied alien children were informed that their grants would not be renewed. Only two of these charities were Catholic groups,” he added.

Desai would not confirm to The Hill if the Catholic Charities in the Archdiocese of Miami was one of those groups.

The Archdiocese of Miami did not immediately respond to The Hill’s request for comment but is expected to host a press conference Thursday afternoon.

data.title: Live updates: Congress grills Kennedy, Vought, Driscoll; Trump announces 10-day Israel-Lebanon truce
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday announced that Israel and Lebanon have agreed to a 10-day ceasefire. He wrote on Truth Social that he had an “excellent conversation” with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Lebanese President Joseph Aoun.

Chair of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Gen. Dan Caine told reporters on Thursday morning that 13 ships have turned around at the U.S. blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. As the war nears the seven-week mark and the ceasefire hits nine days, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth warned Iran it was time to make a deal or face a resumption of U.S. attacks.

A series of Trump officials are on Capitol Hill on Thursday.

On the House side, Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., Army Secretary Dan Driscoll, Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins, U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer and Energy Secretary Chris Wright are testifying.

Kennedy has been grilled on his vaccine stance. Driscoll faced questions on the firing of the Army’s chief of staff.

In the upper chamber, Office of Management and Budget Director Russ Vought is appearing before the Senate, where he’s laid into Democrats on the Department of Homeland Security shutdown.

In New Jersey, voters in the northern portion of the state will head to the polls to decide who will fill Gov. Mikie Sherrill’s (D) former House seat. Here’s what to know.

Trump on Thursday will head to Las Vegas, where he’ll host a roundtable on his “No Tax On Tips” initiative.

Follow live below.

data.title: Harris hammers Trump over gas prices amid 'war of choice'
data.description:
Former Vice President Kamala Harris (D) cast blame on President Trump Wednesday for the surge in gas prices amid the U.S. conflict with Iran, which she called a “war of choice.”

“It’s 15 more dollars every time you fill up a tank of gas,” Harris said outside a gas station in Charlotte, N.C., in a video shared on social platform X.

The fluctuation in fuel prices, she said, would also “carry over to how much you’re paying for all the goods that are being transported on those semis.”

“We’ve got a president who is paying more attention to what he thinks is in his best political interest and personal interest, as opposed to what is in the best interest of working people in America,” the former vice president said.

The national average for a gallon of gas dipped slightly on Thursday to $4.09, according to AAA. The war in the Middle East and the subsequent blockades of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical oil shipping channel in the Gulf, have roiled global oil prices.   

Those prices have cooled some — Brent crude returned to under $100 per barrel on Thursday — since Trump announced a two-week ceasefire with Iran last week, but experts have warned that markets are still on edge about potential supply disruptions.  

Patrick De Haan, the head of petroleum analysis at GasBuddy, wrote in a Monday blog post that motorists should “prepare for another round of price increases.”

“The move toward a full blockade of the Strait of Hormuz is compounding global supply concerns and risks further disrupting flows, which pushed oil prices sharply higher in Sunday night trading,” De Haan said. “As a result, gasoline prices are likely to jump again this week, with diesel expected to follow, until there is a meaningful restoration of shipping through the Strait.”

Gas prices have been inching closer in recent weeks to the record high set in June 2022 amid the Russian invasion of Ukraine, when Harris and former President Biden were still in office.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Wednesday that he was “optimistic” that prices would fall below $3 per gallon sometime between June 20 and September 20.

“It’s bank week here in DC, so I’ve been meeting with a lot of my Middle Eastern counterparts, the finance ministers, and they all say when the straits are open, they can start pumping again within one week,” Bessent told reporters at the White House press briefing.

Democrats, including Harris, have sought to highlight the issue ahead of the November midterms, as affordability concerns remain on voters’ minds.

The former vice president, who lost to Trump in the 2024 presidential election, has used recent appearances to tout her book, “107 Days,” to rip the president’s foreign policy decisions, furthering speculation that she may be eyeing another bid in 2028.

During her stop in Charlotte, she also criticized Trump’s recent attacks on Pope Leo XIV and his posting of an AI-generated image depicting himself as a Jesus-like figure as “blatantly offensive on every level.”

“They’re suggesting that as president and vice president of the United States, they don’t have a responsibility in terms of moral leadership,” Harris said, according to the Charlotte Observer.

data.title: Beshear says Vance forgetting commandments to not worship false idols, tell lies
data.description:
Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear (D) said Vice President Vance has forgotten that worship of “false idols” and support of individuals who tell “lies” is a breach of Catholic law. 

“I think what J.D. Vance is forgetting is the commandment that thou shalt not worship false idols,” Beshear told MS NOW’s Jen Psaki on Wednesday. “Thankfully, he’s a better apologist than he is a peace negotiator, and all he’s doing is defending Donald Trump at all costs.” 

“This is a president who has attacked the pope multiple times, who is just trying to live out and speak the New Testament,” Beshear, who has garnered attention in recent weeks as a potential frontrunner for the 2028 Democratic presidential race, said. “He’s apologizing for a guy who is picturing himself as Jesus and then claiming, ‘No, I thought it was a doctor,’ therefore violating ‘thou shalt not lie.’”

Beshear’s comments come as senior White House officials and Pope Leo XIV have traded criticism on each other’s approach to religion amid the war in Iran. 

The U.S., which first launched joint strikes with Israel on Tehran in February, is approaching the end of a two-week ceasefire deal with the Middle Eastern country.

On Wednesday, Senate Republicans again blocked a resolution to limit the president’s war authority in Iran in accordance with the War Powers Act. 

Vance, a Catholic, has backed Trump’s criticism of the pope in recent weeks including comments where the president accused the pope of being “weak on crime.” Vance argued it wasn’t “newsworthy.” 

He also defended a controversial AI-generated image which depicted Trump as a Jesus-like figure, calling it a “joke.” Trump later took the image down after facing backlash from some of his political supporters and told CBS News he thought the post portrayed him as a doctor. Vance claimed he took it down “because he realized that a lot of people weren’t understanding his humor.” 

On Tuesday, Vance addressed the pope’s opposition to the conflict in Iran directly, warning him to “be careful” when speaking about theology. 

“God does not bless any conflict,” the pope posted on the social platform X last Friday. “Anyone who is a disciple of Christ, the Prince of Peace, is never on the side of those who once wielded the sword and today drop bombs.

“Woe to those who manipulate religion and the very name of God for their own military, economic, and political gain, dragging that which is sacred into darkness and filth,” the pope posted to X on Thursday in an apparent nod to Trump.
